questions.q: The vaccine against the destructive cattle disease rinderpest was developed by which of the following scientists?
questions.exp: Professor Tilahun Yilma is globally renowned for his work in biotechnology, particularly for developing a highly effective, heat-stable recombinant vaccine against rinderpest.

questions.q: Which of the following Ethiopian biologists has been honored for developing high-yielding, drought-tolerant strains of sorghum?
questions.exp: Dr. Gebissa Ejeta won the prestigious World Food Prize in 2009 for his genetic research that led to high-yielding and drought-resistant sorghum varieties.

questions.q: Which of the following Ethiopian biologists has conducted significant research on the effects of chemical pesticides on human health?
questions.exp: Professor Yealemtsehay Mekonnen is widely recognized for her research on environmental physiology and toxicology, focusing on the health impacts of pesticides on agricultural workers.

questions.q: Which scientist discovered that the Endod plant (soapberry) acts as a natural molluscicide to eradicate the snails that transmit bilharzia?
questions.exp: Dr. Aklilu Lemma made the landmark discovery in 1964 that Endod (soapberry) toxins kill the freshwater snails that serve as intermediate hosts for the bilharzia parasite.

questions.q: Which of the following renowned Ethiopian paleoanthropologists discovered the two 160,000-year-old Homo sapiens skulls in Herto, Middle Awash?
questions.exp: Dr. Berhane Asfaw was a co-discoverer of the fossil remains of Homo sapiens idaltu at Herto, which provided crucial evidence for the African origin of modern humans.

questions.q: Which of the following Ethiopian biologists is internationally acclaimed for pioneering work in the conservation of locally adapted native crop seeds (landraces) without the use of chemical fertilizers?
questions.exp: Dr. Melaku Worede is a world-renowned geneticist who established the Ethiopian gene bank, pioneering the conservation of native seed varieties (especially Tef) using organic and traditional farming methods.

questions.q: All of the following are major research areas of the Ethiopian Health and Nutrition Research Institute (EHNRI) EXCEPT:
opts: Nutritional states of mothers and babies
questions.exp: EHNRI focuses on public health, nutrition, and infectious diseases. Forestry and silviculture fall under agricultural and environmental research institutes.

questions.q: Which Ethiopian biological research center is primarily involved in agricultural research and mechanized farming systems?
opts: ALIPB (All Africa Leprosy, Tuberculosis and Rehabilitation Training Centre)
questions.exp: EIAR is the primary national body responsible for generating and scaling agricultural technologies, including mechanized farming.

questions.q: Which of the following research centers in Ethiopia is highly devoted to advanced scientific research related to Leprosy and Tuberculosis?
questions.exp: The Armauer Hansen Research Institute (AHRI) in Addis Ababa was established in 1970 to carry out biomedical research on leprosy and later tuberculosis.

questions.q: Which of the following national institutes is specifically tasked with the conservation and sustainable management of Ethiopia's diverse ecosystems and genetic resources?
questions.exp: The Institute of Biodiversity Conservation (IBC), currently known as the Ethiopian Biodiversity Institute (EBI), manages the country's diverse genetic resources and ecosystems.

questions.q: What is the typical limit of resolution of a standard optical light microscope compared to an electron microscope?
opts: Light microscope: 0.1 mm; Electron microscope: 200 nm
Light microscope: 200 nm; Electron microscope: 0.3 nm
Light microscope: 2000 nm; Electron microscope: 2,000,000 nm
Light microscope: 0.3 nm; Electron microscope: 200 nm
questions.exp: A standard light microscope has a limit of resolution of approximately 200 nm (0.2 micrometers) due to the wavelength of visible light. Transmission electron microscopes use much shorter electron wavelengths, reaching resolutions of about 0.3 nm.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the resolving power of a microscope?
opts: To distinguish two closely spaced structures as separate and clear
To increase the apparent size of the specimen
To adjust the light intensity passing through the condenser
To make the specimen look smaller and more concentrated
questions.exp: Resolving power is the ability of an optical instrument to separate and show details of closely situated objects, whereas magnification only changes image size.

questions.q: In microscopy specimen preparation, which of the following procedures involves the application of chemical dyes to increase contrast in transparent tissues?
questions.exp: Staining uses biological dyes (such as methylene blue or iodine) to bind to specific cell structures, coloring them to provide high visual contrast under light.

questions.q: The laboratory procedure of securing a prepared specimen onto a glass slide for microscopic viewing is known as:
questions.exp: Mounting is the physical preparation technique of placing a specimen on a glass slide under a coverslip for protection and preservation.

questions.q: Cells that undergo structural and biochemical modifications to carry out specific functions in a multicellular organism are called:
questions.exp: Cell specialization (differentiation) is the process by which generic cells change into specific cells suited to perform distinct physiological tasks.

questions.q: A eukaryotic cell containing an exceptionally high density of mitochondria is adapted to primarily do what?
opts: Produce large amounts of digestive enzymes
Perform a high rate of photosynthesis
Synthesize a large amount of ATP
questions.exp: Mitochondria are the sites of aerobic cellular respiration, which couples electron transport to synthesize ATP.

questions.q: Which of the following cellular structures is directly involved in the translation stage of protein synthesis?
questions.exp: Ribosomes read the genetic code on messenger RNA (mRNA) and catalyze the formation of peptide bonds between amino acids.

questions.q: Which of the following organelles contains thylakoid membranes and acts as the site of photosynthesis?
questions.exp: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll pigments organized into photosystems embedded in thylakoid membranes, which capture solar energy.

questions.q: The passive net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential to a region of lower water potential across a selectively permeable membrane is:
questions.exp: Osmosis is a specialized form of diffusion restricted to the movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable barrier.

questions.q: Which of the following statements is true regarding the physical factors that affect the rate of simple diffusion?
opts: Diffusion rates increase as thermal energy decreases
Diffusion rates increase as thermal energy increases
Diffusion rates decrease when there is a steeper concentration gradient
Diffusion rates increase when the concentration gradient is horizontal
questions.exp: An increase in temperature increases the kinetic energy of particles, making them move faster and collide more frequently, which increases the rate of diffusion.

questions.q: The movement of solute particles from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration down a concentration gradient is called:
questions.exp: Diffusion is the passive net movement of particles from high to low concentration due to their random thermal motion.

questions.q: If a living animal cell is placed in a highly concentrated, hypertonic salt solution, what physical change will be observed?
opts: Water will enter the cell and cause it to lyse
The cell will shrink and shrivel
Salt will move actively into the cell
questions.exp: Because animal cells lack a rigid cell wall, water leaves the cytoplasm via osmosis, causing the cell to shrink. Plasmolysis occurs only in walled plant cells.

questions.q: The most common and fundamental monosaccharide monomer of carbohydrates is:
questions.exp: Glucose (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>12</sub>O<sub>6</sub>) is the most abundant monosaccharide in nature and serves as the primary building block for structural and storage polysaccharides.

questions.q: When 50 amino acid molecules are joined together in a linear polypeptide chain through condensation reactions, how many peptide bonds are formed and water molecules are released respectively?
questions.exp: In a linear polypeptide chain, the number of peptide bonds formed and water molecules released is always (N - 1), where N is the number of monomers. For 50 amino acids, this is 49 bonds and 49 water molecules.

questions.q: Unsaturated fatty acids differ from saturated fatty acids because unsaturated fatty acids:
opts: Have all single carbon-to-carbon bonds in their chain
Contain one or more double carbon-to-carbon bonds
Are derived exclusively from animal storage tissues
questions.exp: Unsaturated fatty acids contain one or more double bonds (C=C), which introduce kinks into the hydrocarbon tail, keeping them liquid at room temperature.

questions.q: Which of the following minerals is a critical structural component of bones and teeth and is also needed for muscle contraction?
questions.exp: Calcium is stored in bone tissue as calcium phosphate. It is also a key regulator of cellular processes like muscle contraction and nerve impulse transmission.

questions.q: Which of the following vitamins is essential for collagen synthesis and the structural integrity of connective tissues?
questions.exp: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) acts as an essential cofactor for the enzymes that stabilize the triple helix structure of collagen.

questions.q: Water is vital for living organisms. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding its physiological role?
opts: It acts as a universal solvent for biochemical reactions
It is involved in transporting metabolic wastes and nutrients
It regulates internal body temperature through evaporative cooling
It is completely inert and does not participate in any metabolic reactions
questions.exp: Water is chemically active; it participates directly in many key metabolic reactions, including hydrolysis reactions that break down macromolecules.

questions.q: Which of the following components of plant food is non-digestible by humans but acts as essential roughage in a balanced diet?
questions.exp: Humans lack the enzyme cellulase needed to break down beta-1,4-glycosidic bonds in cellulose. It remains undigested, providing dietary fiber that helps move food through the gut.

questions.q: Which of the following sequences represents the correct order of processes in the human digestive system?
questions.exp: Food must be taken in (ingestion), broken down (digestion), taken into the bloodstream (absorption), used by cells (assimilation), and undigested waste must be removed (egestion).

questions.q: To confirm the presence of a reducing sugar like glucose in a food sample, which test and positive result are correct?
opts: Benedict's test resulting in an orange-red precipitate
Biuret test resulting in a deep purple color
Emulsion test resulting in a cloudy white layer
Iodine test resulting in a blue-black color
questions.exp: Benedict's reagent contains copper(II) sulfate. Reducing sugars reduce the copper(II) ions to copper(I) oxide, forming a brick-red or orange precipitate upon heating.

questions.q: Which of the following factors alters the rate of an enzyme-catalyzed reaction without permanently denaturing the enzyme protein?
questions.exp: Low temperatures reduce the kinetic energy of enzymes and substrates, slowing down molecular collisions. This slows the reaction rate reversibly without breaking the hydrogen bonds that hold the enzyme's structure together.

questions.q: Which of the following human tooth types is INCORRECTLY matched with its primary function?
questions.exp: Canines are sharp and pointed, adapted for tearing and gripping food rather than chewing and crushing.

questions.q: Why is a food sample boiled with hydrochloric acid (HCl) before performing Benedict's test for non-reducing sugars like sucrose?
opts: To neutralize basic compounds in the sample
To prevent the denaturing of Benedict's reagent
To reduce the blue copper ions to red copper oxide
To hydrolyze the glycosidic bonds in non-reducing sugars
questions.exp: Boiling with acid hydrolyzes the glycosidic bonds in non-reducing disaccharides like sucrose, breaking them down into reducing monosaccharides (glucose and fructose) that can react with Benedict's reagent.

questions.q: Which of the following deficient nutrients is correctly paired with its corresponding deficiency disease?
questions.exp: Vitamin A (retinol) is a precursor for rhodopsin, the visual pigment in rod cells. A deficiency in Vitamin A directly impairs low-light vision, causing night blindness.

questions.q: During exhalation (breathing out) in humans, which of the following physical changes does NOT occur?
opts: The volume of the thoracic cavity decreases
The air pressure inside the chest cavity decreases
The diaphragm relaxes and moves upward
The air pressure inside the lungs increases
questions.exp: During exhalation, the thoracic cavity volume decreases, which compresses the lungs and *increases* the air pressure inside them above atmospheric pressure, forcing air out.

questions.q: The muscular partition that separates the thoracic (chest) cavity from the abdominal cavity is called the:
questions.exp: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped sheet of skeletal muscle that forms the floor of the thoracic cavity and plays a central role in breathing.

questions.q: Which of the following represents the correct sequence of anatomical structures that air passes through during inhalation?
opts: Nasal cavity → Trachea → Bronchus → Bronchioles → Alveolus
Alveolus → Bronchus → Bronchioles → Trachea → Nasal cavity
Alveolus → Bronchioles → Bronchus → Trachea → Nasal cavity
Nasal cavity → Alveolus → Bronchus → Trachea → Bronchioles
questions.exp: Inhaled air enters the nasal cavity, travels down the trachea, enters the primary bronchi, branches into smaller bronchioles, and terminates in the alveoli where gas exchange occurs.

questions.q: While oxygen is transported primarily inside red blood cells, most digested nutrients and metabolic wastes are transported dissolved in the:
questions.exp: Blood plasma is the liquid component of blood, consisting of about 90% water, which dissolves and transports nutrients, hormones, CO<sub>2</sub>, and metabolic wastes.

questions.q: The maximum volume of air a person can expire following a maximum inhalation is called:
questions.exp: Vital capacity is the sum of tidal volume, inspiratory reserve volume, and expiratory reserve volume, representing the maximum volume of exchangeable air.

questions.q: The heart valve that regulates the one-way flow of blood from the right atrium into the right ventricle is the:
questions.exp: The tricuspid valve, named for its three flaps, prevents the backflow of blood into the right atrium when the right ventricle contracts.

questions.q: Which of the following blood vessels carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs?
questions.exp: Unlike other arteries, the pulmonary artery carries oxygen-poor (deoxygenated) blood away from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.q: Which human blood group is referred to as the 'universal recipient' because its red blood cells carry both A and B antigens?
questions.exp: Individuals with blood group AB produce neither anti-A nor anti-B antibodies in their plasma, allowing them to receive blood transfusions from any ABO blood group.

questions.q: All of the following are lifestyle and physiological risk factors that increase the likelihood of developing hypertension EXCEPT:
questions.exp: Reducing dietary sodium intake is a preventative measure that lowers blood volume and blood pressure, reducing the risk of hypertension.

questions.q: The design of Eastgate Centre in Zimbabwe, which uses passive ventilation to maintain a stable indoor temperature, was inspired by which of the following natural structures?
questions.exp: Termite mounds maintain stable internal temperatures despite extreme outdoor weather through a system of passive ventilation chimneys, a design copied in bionic architecture.

questions.q: Which of the following fields of science focuses on studying and copying functional structures and processes from nature to solve human engineering challenges?
questions.exp: Biomimicry (or bionics) is the practice of looking to nature's time-tested designs to create sustainable and efficient human technologies.

questions.q: In bioethics, the evaluation of a biological procedure or discovery focuses on assessing:
opts: The commercial profit margins of the discovery
The ethical implications, rightness, or wrongness of its application
How quickly the technology can be manufactured
Whether the research was funded by public or private organizations
questions.exp: Bioethics is the study of ethical issues emerging from advances in biology and medicine, focusing on moral responsibility and human welfare.

questions.q: What is the biological term for the balanced arrangement of an animal's body parts around a central point or axis?
questions.exp: Body symmetry refers to how an animal's body parts are arranged around a central axis, categorized as radial, bilateral, or asymmetrical.

questions.q: Which of the following is a key distinguishing characteristic of invertebrate animals?
opts: They possess a closed circulatory system with multiple hearts
They develop an internal bony skeleton
They are strictly endothermic and maintain a constant body temperature
They lack a cartilaginous or bony vertebral column (backbone)
questions.exp: Invertebrates are defined by the absence of a backbone, making up over 95% of all animal species on Earth.

questions.q: The reproductive strategy in which the young develop inside the mother's body, receiving direct nourishment from her blood through a placenta before birth, is:
questions.exp: Viviparity is live birth, where the embryo develops internally and receives nutrients directly from the mother via placental connections.

questions.q: In some insect species, individuals can develop from unfertilized eggs without sperm fertilization. This asexual reproductive process is:
questions.exp: Parthenogenesis is a form of asexual reproduction in which growth and development of embryos occur without fertilization.

questions.q: Which of the following groups of organisms reproduces using external fertilization?
questions.exp: Most amphibians release their gametes directly into the water, where fertilization takes place outside the parents' bodies.

questions.q: Why is temperature considered an environmental factor that determines the sex of offspring during the incubation of crocodile eggs?
opts: Higher temperatures destroy the female gametes
Temperature alters the genetic sequence of the embryo's DNA
questions.exp: Crocodiles undergo temperature-dependent sex determination (TSD), where the temperature during a critical window of incubation determines the sex of the offspring, rather than sex chromosomes.

questions.q: The avian mating system in which a single female mates with multiple males during a single breeding season is called:
questions.exp: Polyandry is a specific class of polygamy where one female forms mating pairs with more than one male at the same time.

questions.q: During embryonic development, the phase in which the three germ layers reorganize and differentiate to form specific organs is:
questions.exp: Organogenesis is the period of development where the ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm give rise to the internal organs of the organism.

questions.q: All of the following are ecological benefits of insects in agriculture EXCEPT:
opts: Acting as effective pollinators for flowering crops
Serving as biological control agents against weed species
Functioning as vectors that transmit viral plant diseases
Decomposing organic matter to return nutrients to the soil
questions.exp: Acting as vectors for plant pathogens is a harmful agricultural impact of insects, causing crop damage and reducing yields.

questions.q: An animal behavior pattern where an individual stops responding to a repeated stimulus that is neither beneficial nor harmful is called:
questions.exp: Habituation is a simple form of learning in which an animal filters out unimportant, repetitive stimuli, allowing it to focus on more critical environmental cues.

questions.q: The behavior of both male and female wolves in a pack patrolling and marking their territory with scent markings containing pheromones is an example of:
questions.exp: Territorial behavior is the defense and marking of a physical area against other members of the same species or competing packs.

questions.q: A human quickly pulling their hand away from a hot stove without conscious thought is an example of which type of behavior?
questions.exp: A reflex is an involuntary, rapid, and unlearned response to a stimulus, controlled by a simple neural pathway called a reflex arc.

questions.q: An increase in blood glucose causes the pancreas to secrete insulin, which promotes glucose storage and lowers blood sugar back to baseline. This physiological regulation is an example of:
questions.exp: Negative feedback is a regulatory mechanism in which a stimulus triggers a response that counteracts and reverses the initial change, maintaining homeostasis.

questions.q: Which of the following characteristics explains the highly specific nature of enzymes?
opts: They are destroyed by moderate changes in thermal energy
They can catalyze multiple unrelated chemical reactions
They have an active site with a shape that matches only specific substrate molecules
They alter the equilibrium constant of a chemical reaction
questions.exp: The three-dimensional conformation of an enzyme's active site is highly complementary to its specific substrate, similar to a lock and key.

questions.q: The specific level of protein structure characterized by the three-dimensional folding and bending of a single polypeptide chain is the:
questions.exp: Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide, stabilized by interactions between R-groups, such as disulfide bridges and hydrogen bonds.

questions.q: The enzyme-substrate binding model that proposes the active site shifts slightly to fit snugly around the substrate upon binding is the:
questions.exp: The induced-fit model suggests that the enzyme's active site is flexible and changes shape slightly to maximize binding stability when it interacts with the substrate.

questions.q: An enzyme inhibitor that directly competes with the substrate for binding at the active site is classified as a:
questions.exp: Competitive inhibitors mimic the substrate's shape, allowing them to bind to the active site and block the substrate from entering.

questions.q: An active, complete, and functional enzyme consisting of an apoenzyme combined with its necessary cofactor is called a:
questions.exp: A holoenzyme is the active, combined form of an enzyme. The protein part alone is the inactive apoenzyme, which requires a non-protein cofactor to function.

questions.q: Which of the following statements is true regarding allosteric feedback inhibition?
opts: The final end-product binds directly to the active site of the first enzyme
The final end-product binds to an allosteric site on the first enzyme, changing its shape
The active site remains unchanged throughout the process
The process cannot regulate metabolic pathway speed
questions.exp: In feedback inhibition, the end-product of a pathway binds to an allosteric site on an enzyme near the start of the pathway, changing its active site shape and halting production.

questions.q: Which of the following enzymes is used commercially in the food and beverage industry to clarify fruit juices?
questions.exp: Pectinases break down pectin, a structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls. This helps extract more juice and reduces cloudiness to clarify the product.

questions.q: Which of the following organic molecules, essential as enzyme cofactors, are primarily derived from dietary vitamins?
questions.exp: Coenzymes are organic non-protein molecules, such as NAD<sup>+</sup> and FAD, that transfer chemical groups during catalysis and are often synthesized from vitamins.

questions.q: In an enzyme-catalyzed reaction, what happens to the enzyme molecules at the end of the reaction?
opts: They are converted into final products
They are permanently denatured by the reaction
They remain unchanged and can be used again in another reaction
They are broken down into amino acids
questions.exp: Enzymes act as catalysts. They speed up reactions without being consumed or permanently altered, allowing them to be reused.

questions.q: The process of heating germinated grain to stop growth while preserving key starch-digesting enzymes is known as:
questions.exp: Kilning uses heat to dry the germinated grain (malt), stopping growth and development while locking in the enzymes needed for brewing.

questions.q: Which agricultural pruning practice involves cutting a tree's main stem close to ground level to stimulate the growth of multiple new shoots?
questions.exp: Coppicing is a traditional woodland management method that exploits the capacity of many broadleaved trees to put out new shoots from their stumps after being cut down.

questions.q: The first vitamin produced on an industrial scale using microbial fermentation was:
questions.exp: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is synthesized exclusively by microbes. It was the first vitamin produced commercially using large-scale bacterial fermentation.

questions.q: Which of the following terms describes the state where all members of a population have physical, social, and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food?
questions.exp: Food security is defined by the FAO as reliable access to a sufficient quantity of affordable, nutritious food.

questions.q: Modern biosensors can be designed to detect all of the following biological and environmental agents EXCEPT:
questions.exp: Biosensors are designed to detect trace biological targets, pathogens, or toxins. Inert nitrogen gas, making up 78% of the atmosphere, does not require biosensing.

questions.q: All of the following statements are correct regarding genetic fingerprinting EXCEPT:
opts: It is primarily based on analyzing ribosomal RNA sequences
It produces unique profiles for every individual except identical twins
It utilizes gel electrophoresis to separate amplified DNA fragments
It is widely used in forensic science and paternity testing
questions.exp: Genetic fingerprinting analyzes genomic DNA, specifically targeting highly variable regions like Short Tandem Repeats (STRs), rather than ribosomal RNA.

questions.q: Which of the following is a key structural characteristic of bacterial cells?
opts: Their genetic material is contained within a membrane-bound nucleus
They are typically larger than eukaryotic cells
They contain membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria
Their circular DNA is located directly in the cytoplasm
questions.exp: Bacteria are prokaryotes. They lack a membrane-bound nucleus and other internal organelles, meaning their DNA sits freely in the nucleoid region of the cytoplasm.

questions.q: Which of the following groups of organisms is classified as extremophilic Archaea capable of thriving in high-temperature environments like hydrothermal vents?
questions.exp: Hyperthermophiles are specialized microbes that thrive in extremely hot environments, typically above 80 degrees Celsius.

questions.q: Which of the following fungal species is used as a biological assay to detect micronutrients like Magnesium, Copper, and Zinc in soil samples?
questions.exp: Aspergillus niger is extremely sensitive to trace metals in its growth medium, making it a reliable bioassay organism to measure bioavailable copper, zinc, and other micronutrients in soil.

questions.q: Which of the following flagellated protozoan parasites is the causative agent of Chagas disease?
questions.exp: Trypanosoma cruzi is the protozoan that causes Chagas disease, transmitted to humans through the bites of triatomine bugs (kissing bugs).

questions.q: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding the lysogenic cycle of a bacteriophage?
opts: The viral DNA integrates into the host bacterial chromosome as a prophage
The viral DNA remains separate and does not integrate into the host genome
The host cell survives and replicates the viral DNA during division
It allows the virus to remain dormant for many generations
questions.exp: In the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA must integrate into the host's genome to be replicated along with the host cell's DNA.

questions.q: Which class of viral capsid symmetry is characterized by a structure with 20 triangular faces and 12 corners?
questions.exp: An icosahedron is a regular polyhedron with 20 equilateral triangular faces and 12 vertices, a common geometric capsid shape among viruses.

questions.q: Which of the following soil-dwelling bacteria is widely used as a biological pesticide because it produces delta-endotoxin proteins that target insect pests?
questions.exp: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) produces crystal proteins (cry toxins) during sporulation, which are highly toxic to specific insect larvae but safe for other organisms.

questions.q: All of the following statements are correct about catabolic pathways EXCEPT:
opts: They break down complex molecules into simpler compounds
They represent the constructive, building phase of metabolism
questions.exp: Anabolism is the constructive phase of metabolism where simpler molecules are built up into complex ones. Catabolism is the destructive, breaking-down phase.

questions.q: In which compartment of the chloroplast do the light-independent reactions of photosynthesis take place?
questions.exp: The light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle), which use carbon dioxide to synthesize sugars, take place in the stroma (the fluid-filled space of the chloroplast).

questions.q: Which of the following photosynthetic pigments is primary and is directly involved in converting solar energy into chemical energy?
questions.exp: Chlorophyll a is the primary photosynthetic pigment found in the reaction centers of photosystems, while others act as accessory pigments that pass captured light energy to it.

questions.q: Which photosynthetic pigment is found in members of the Chromista and Dinoflagellates, but not in green plants?
questions.exp: Chlorophyll c is an accessory pigment found in certain marine algae, including diatoms, brown algae, and dinoflagellates, but is absent in land plants.

questions.q: The reaction center of Photosystem II (PS II) consists of a specialized pair of chlorophyll a molecules with an absorption peak at which wavelength?
questions.exp: Photosystem II contains reaction center chlorophyll molecules known as P680 because they absorb light most efficiently at a wavelength of 680 nm.

questions.q: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding the light-independent reactions of photosynthesis?
opts: They are mediated by enzyme catalysts
They take place at a slower rate than the light reactions
They represent the first step in capturing solar energy
They occur in the stroma of the chloroplast
questions.exp: The light-dependent reactions occur first, capturing solar energy to produce ATP and NADPH. The light-independent reactions use these products later to fix carbon.

questions.q: During the light-independent reactions (Calvin Cycle) of photosynthesis, which key metabolic process occurs?
opts: Conversion of solar energy to ATP
questions.exp: Carbon fixation is the process where inorganic carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) is incorporated into organic compounds, catalyzed by the enzyme RuBisCO.

questions.q: The first stage of cellular respiration, which takes place in the cytosol and breaks down glucose in the absence of oxygen, is called:
questions.exp: Glycolysis is an anaerobic pathway that breaks down one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate, generating a net yield of 2 ATP and 2 NADH.

questions.q: What is the net yield of ATP molecules generated per molecule of glucose during glycolysis?
questions.exp: While 4 ATP molecules are produced during the energy-payoff phase of glycolysis, 2 ATP are consumed during the preparatory phase, resulting in a net yield of 2 ATP.

questions.q: In the mitochondrial matrix, pyruvic acid reacts with Coenzyme A during the link reaction to form which molecule?
questions.exp: The link reaction decarboxylates and oxidizes pyruvate to form a two-carbon acetyl group, which binds to Coenzyme A to form Acetyl CoA.

questions.q: The biochemical pathway that completely oxidizes the acetyl group of Acetyl CoA to carbon dioxide in the mitochondrial matrix is:
questions.exp: The Krebs cycle (or citric acid cycle) is a series of chemical reactions that oxidizes Acetyl CoA to generate NADH, FADH<sub>2</sub>, and ATP, releasing CO<sub>2</sub> as a byproduct.

questions.q: What is the final electron acceptor at the end of the electron transport chain in aerobic cellular respiration?
questions.exp: Oxygen acts as the terminal electron acceptor, combining with electrons and protons (H<sup>+</sup>) to form water, which prevents the transport chain from backing up.

questions.q: Where in the mitochondrion are the electron transport chain carriers and ATP synthase enzymes located?
questions.exp: The inner mitochondrial membrane is folded into cristae to maximize surface area, housing the electron transport chain complexes and ATP synthase.

questions.q: Lactic acid fermentation differs from alcoholic fermentation because lactic acid fermentation:
opts: Does not produce carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) as a byproduct
Does not require the oxidation of NADH
Occurs only in yeast and plant cells
questions.exp: Lactic acid fermentation reduces pyruvate directly to lactate without releasing CO<sub>2</sub>. Alcoholic fermentation decarboxylates pyruvate first, releasing CO<sub>2</sub> and forming acetaldehyde before reducing it to ethanol.

questions.q: The invention of Velcro, which uses tiny hooks to attach to loops, was inspired by which of the following natural structures?
questions.exp: In 1941, George de Mestral examined cockleburs that stuck to his dog's fur, noticing tiny hooks on the seed pods, which inspired the invention of Velcro.

questions.q: The aerodynamic shape and wing profile of modern commercial aircraft are inspired primarily by the flight mechanics of which animals?
questions.exp: The wing cross-section (airfoil) of modern aircraft mimics the wings of birds, designed to generate lift by manipulating air pressure gradients.

questions.q: All of the following are key conditions and assumptions required for a population to remain in Hardy-Weinberg genetic equilibrium EXCEPT:
opts: A small population size susceptible to genetic drift
No selection pressures favoring specific genotypes
No mutation or migration introducing new alleles
questions.exp: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires an infinitely large population to prevent random changes in allele frequencies (genetic drift).

questions.q: Which part of a neuron is specialized to receive incoming signals from sensory receptors or other neurons?
questions.exp: Dendrites are highly branched, tree-like projections extending from the cell body that receive neurotransmitter signals from adjacent cells.

questions.q: Which of the following events occurs during the depolarization phase of an action potential?
opts: Potassium channels open, and potassium ions rush out
Sodium channels open, and sodium ions rush into the cell
Calcium channels open, and calcium is pumped out
The inside of the cell becomes more negative than the resting potential
questions.exp: Depolarization is triggered when voltage-gated sodium channels open, allowing sodium ions (Na<sup>+</sup>) to rush down their electrochemical gradient into the axon, making the interior positive.

questions.q: Which of the following thermal physiological responses is triggered by the brain's control center to lower body temperature when it rises above normal?
opts: Vasodilation and activation of sweat glands to allow evaporative cooling
Inactivation of sweat glands and skeletal muscle contraction
A positive feedback cascade that further increases temperature
questions.exp: To dissipate excess heat, the hypothalamus triggers vasodilation (dilating skin blood vessels to release heat) and activates sweat glands to promote evaporative cooling.

questions.q: Which physical property of enzymes is characterized by their inability to pass through a semipermeable membrane during dialysis due to their high molecular weights?
questions.exp: Due to their large size, enzyme molecules are colloidal. They form stable suspensions in water and cannot dialyze (diffuse through a semipermeable membrane), unlike small ions or molecules.

questions.q: Which of the following regulatory mechanisms involves the enzyme-catalyzed addition or removal of a functional group (such as a phosphate) to covalently alter a protein's active state?
questions.exp: Covalent modification is the reversible addition or removal of a chemical group (most commonly phosphorylation/dephosphorylation catalyzed by kinases/phosphatases) to switch an enzyme between active and inactive states.
